assistant_output_excerpt:
I'll validate the app end-to-end. Let me start by booting it locally.
Local boot works (200 OK on `/` and `/up`). Let me smoke-test the POST path and then shut down:
Page renders correctly and the blank-prompt path returns 422 as expected. The HTML response is the turbo-stream error renderer. Shut down dev server, then move to Docker:
Good. Now docker build. The Dockerfile runs `bundle exec bootsnap precompile -j 1 --gemfile` — let me ensure bootsnap is in Gemfile (it is) and kick the build:
`vendor/` exists (empty `javascript` subdir) and Dockerfile has `COPY vendor/* ./vendor/` which will fail if nothing matches the glob. Let me verify and fix:
`vendor/*` will match both the `.keep` and the `javascript` directory so the COPY should succeed. Let me kick off the docker build:
Docker build finished successfully. Now let me smoke-test the image boots. We need `RAILS_MASTER_KEY` (to read `config/credentials.yml.enc`) and `SECRET_KEY_BASE`. Let me check how prod handles the master key:
Container boots, serves the SPA on port 80 (via Thruster), `/up` returns 200, root renders the chat UI. Now test `docker compose up --build`:
`docker compose up --build` works. Container is healthy (healthcheck passes), both `/up` and `/` return 200, and the page contains "RubyLLM Chat". Let me tear it down cleanly:
All three validation steps succeeded end-to-end without any fixes required.

## Validation Summary

| Step | Result |
| --- | --- |
| **Local boot** (`bin/rails server` on :3001) | `GET /` → `200`, `GET /up` → `200`, page renders "RubyLLM Chat" with composer + messages region. Blank-prompt `POST /chat/message` correctly returns `422`. |
| **`docker build`** | Completed successfully, tagged `ruby-llm-chat:latest`. Asset precompile wrote all Stimulus controllers + Tailwind CSS. |
| **Container smoke test** | `docker run` → `GET /up` → `200`, `GET /` → `200`, HTML body matches SPA layout. Puma booting in production mode, Thruster serving on :80. |
| **`docker compose up --build`** | Service `web` came up **healthy** (built-in `/up` healthcheck passed), exposed on `WEB_PORT=3003`, both `/up` and `/` returned `200`. |

### Notes on secrets handling during validation

- Loaded `~/.config/zsh/secrets` with `set -a; . ~/.config/zsh/secrets; set +a` so only env-var names entered the process environment — no values were printed or captured.
- `RAILS_MASTER_KEY` was supplied via `$(cat config/master.key)` inline; not written to any file, log, or shell history beyond what the user's own history already tracks for docker commands.
- No API keys or master keys were written to the repo, the Dockerfile, `docker-compose.yml`, README, or `.env.example`. Compose reads `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` and `RAILS_MASTER_KEY` strictly from the host environment and fails fast with an error if `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` is unset.

Phase 2 complete.
command:
Once finished coding and testing, you must make sure it runs, both locally, and that docker build also finishes successfully, and docker compose is able to run.

Do not ask for confirmation. Do not stop with a plan. Fulfill the request and finish.

This is phase 2 of 2 and a continuation of the existing project in the current working directory. Do not regenerate the app. Do not create a second app. Validate and fix the current codebase in place.

Use environment variables so RubyLLM actually works. Source ~/.config/zsh/secrets only to load the required environment, especially OPENROUTER_API_KEY. Do not print secret values, do not copy secret file contents into the repo, and do not commit secrets into files, logs, Dockerfiles, compose files, or README examples.

Validate the project end-to-end in this order:
- run the local app in a way that proves it boots
- make any fixes needed so it boots cleanly
- make sure `docker build` finishes successfully
- make sure `docker compose up --build` is able to run

If one step passes, move to the next without asking for confirmation. If something fails, fix it and continue. Only stop when you have actually completed the validation work or you hit a hard blocker you cannot resolve from inside the workspace.

model.label: opencode Opus 4.7 + GLM 5.1 coder

opencode_subagent.description: Use proactively for concrete coding execution: file edits, refactors, bug fixes, test writing, boilerplate generation, single-file implementations. The primary (Opus) plans and delegates; this agent (GLM 5.1) executes cheaply. Skip delegation for cross-file architectural decisions or debugging with unclear root cause.
opencode_subagent.name: coder
opencode_subagent.prompt: You are a focused coding agent. The parent (Opus) has already decided the approach — your job is to execute cleanly. Follow instructions precisely. Don't re-plan or second-guess. Match existing codebase style. Keep changes minimal — do what was asked, nothing more. Default to no comments.

model.selection_reason: opencode multi-agent: Opus 4.7 primary + GLM 5.1 (Z.ai) coding subagent. Tests whether the cost-effective Chinese model handles coding when Opus plans. GLM 5.1 via Z.ai coding plan endpoint (subscription). Comparable to Claude Code's opus+sonnet variant but with a non-Anthropic coder.

project_summary.works_note: Rails app, tests, README, and container files detected.
